01-03.text: Alaska was admitted as the 49th state, changing representation in Congress and the Electoral College.

01-04.text: Nancy Pelosi became the first woman to serve as Speaker of the House.

01-06.text: President Franklin D. Roosevelt delivered the Four Freedoms speech to Congress.
01-06.ap_connection: Presidential agenda setting; civil liberties; foreign policy

01-06.text: Congress reconvened after the January 6 attack and certified the Electoral College results.
01-06.ap_connection: Electoral College; peaceful transfer of power; Congress

01-10.text: Thomas Paine published Common Sense, arguing for American independence and popular sovereignty.
01-10.ap_connection: Popular sovereignty; political participation; founding ideals

01-15.text: Martin Luther King Jr. was born; his leadership later helped push federal civil rights action.
01-15.ap_connection: Civil rights movement; social movements; federal legislation

01-20.text: The first presidential inauguration under the 20th Amendment was held, moving Inauguration Day from March to January.

01-22.text: The Supreme Court decided Roe v. Wade, recognizing a constitutional right to abortion under privacy doctrine.
01-22.ap_connection: Civil liberties; privacy; substantive due process

01-24.text: The 24th Amendment was ratified, banning poll taxes in federal elections.

01-30.text: Franklin D. Roosevelt was born; his presidency reshaped expectations of federal power during the New Deal.

02-01.text: Four students began the Greensboro sit-ins, a major civil rights protest against segregation.
02-01.ap_connection: Civil rights; linkage institutions; social movements

02-03.text: The 15th Amendment was ratified, prohibiting voting discrimination based on race, color, or previous condition of servitude.
02-03.ap_connection: Voting rights; Reconstruction amendments; political participation

02-03.text: The 16th Amendment was ratified, authorizing Congress to collect a federal income tax.

02-08.text: The Boy Scouts of America was incorporated, later becoming part of civic life and civil society debates.

02-12.text: Abraham Lincoln was born; his presidency tested executive power, federalism, and constitutional union.

02-19.text: President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed Executive Order 9066, authorizing Japanese American internment during World War II.

02-24.text: The Supreme Court decided Marbury v. Madison, establishing judicial review.
02-24.ap_connection: Judicial review; Supreme Court; checks and balances

02-27.text: The 22nd Amendment was ratified, limiting presidents to two elected terms.
02-27.ap_connection: Presidency; constitutional amendments; checks on power

03-01.text: The Articles of Confederation went into effect, creating the first national government of the United States.
03-01.ap_connection: Articles of Confederation; federalism; weaknesses of national government

03-04.text: The Constitution took effect as the framework for the new federal government.

03-06.text: The Supreme Court decided Dred Scott v. Sandford, denying citizenship to enslaved and formerly enslaved Black people.
03-06.ap_connection: Civil rights; Supreme Court; constitutional conflict

03-12.text: Franklin D. Roosevelt gave his first fireside chat, using radio to build public support for banking reforms.

03-15.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson addressed Congress after Selma and called for voting rights legislation.

03-18.text: The Supreme Court decided Gideon v. Wainwright, requiring states to provide attorneys for defendants who cannot afford one in felony cases.
03-18.ap_connection: Due process; selective incorporation; rights of the accused

03-22.text: Congress sent the Equal Rights Amendment to the states for ratification, though it did not become part of the Constitution.

03-26.text: The Supreme Court decided Baker v. Carr, opening the door to federal court review of legislative districting.

04-02.text: President Woodrow Wilson asked Congress for a declaration of war against Germany.

04-04.text: Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated, intensifying national debate over civil rights, protest, and federal action.
04-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; social movements; public policy

04-09.text: Congress passed the Civil Rights Act of 1866 over President Andrew Johnson's veto.

04-13.text: Thomas Jefferson was born; his ideas shaped the Declaration of Independence and debates over limited government.
04-13.ap_connection: Declaration of Independence; natural rights; limited government

04-17.text: The Supreme Court decided Lochner v. New York, limiting some economic regulations under liberty-of-contract reasoning.
04-17.ap_connection: Judicial review; economic liberty; due process

04-19.text: The Battles of Lexington and Concord began armed conflict between colonists and British authority.
04-19.ap_connection: Revolutionary origins; consent of the governed

04-30.text: George Washington took the first presidential oath of office in New York City.

05-04.text: The Freedom Rides began, challenging segregation in interstate bus travel.
05-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; interstate commerce; federal enforcement

05-17.text: The Supreme Court decided Brown v. Board of Education, ruling school segregation unconstitutional.
05-17.ap_connection: Equal protection; civil rights; Supreme Court

05-18.text: The Supreme Court decided Plessy v. Ferguson, approving 'separate but equal' segregation.

05-25.text: The Constitutional Convention opened in Philadelphia.

06-02.text: The Indian Citizenship Act granted U.S. citizenship to Native Americans born in the United States.

06-08.text: James Madison introduced proposed amendments that would become the Bill of Rights.
06-08.ap_connection: Bill of Rights; civil liberties; Federalist/Anti-Federalist debate

06-13.text: The Supreme Court decided Miranda v. Arizona, requiring warnings before custodial interrogation.
06-13.ap_connection: Rights of the accused; due process; selective incorporation

06-15.text: The 12th Amendment was ratified, changing the Electoral College process for president and vice president.

06-19.text: Union troops announced emancipation in Galveston, Texas, an event later commemorated as Juneteenth.

06-23.text: Title IX was enacted, banning sex discrimination in federally funded education programs.
06-23.ap_connection: Civil rights; federal policy; equal protection

06-25.text: The Supreme Court decided Engel v. Vitale, ruling school-sponsored prayer unconstitutional.
06-25.ap_connection: Establishment Clause; civil liberties; selective incorporation

06-26.text: The Supreme Court decided Obergefell v. Hodges, requiring states to license and recognize same-sex marriages.
06-26.ap_connection: Equal protection; due process; civil rights

07-02.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson signed the Civil Rights Act of 1964.

07-04.text: The Second Continental Congress adopted the Declaration of Independence.
07-04.ap_connection: Natural rights; popular sovereignty; founding ideals

07-06.text: An early Republican Party convention met in Jackson, Michigan, reflecting growing conflict over slavery and the future of national politics.

07-09.text: The 14th Amendment was ratified, defining citizenship and protecting due process and equal protection.
07-09.ap_connection: Civil rights; due process; equal protection

07-16.text: Congress approved the Residence Act, establishing the future permanent capital on the Potomac River.

07-26.text: President Harry Truman ordered the desegregation of the U.S. armed forces.

07-30.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson signed Medicare and Medicaid into law.
07-30.ap_connection: Social welfare policy; Congress; federal bureaucracy

08-02.text: Calvin Coolidge became president after the death of Warren G. Harding.

08-06.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson signed the Voting Rights Act of 1965.

08-09.text: Richard Nixon resigned the presidency, and Gerald Ford became president.
08-09.ap_connection: Presidency; checks and balances; impeachment pressure

08-18.text: The 19th Amendment was ratified, prohibiting voting discrimination based on sex.

08-24.text: British forces burned Washington, D.C., during the War of 1812.
08-24.ap_connection: National government; war powers; federal institutions

08-28.text: The March on Washington brought civil rights activists to the capital and featured Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I Have a Dream' speech.

09-02.text: Congress created the Department of the Treasury.

09-05.text: The First Continental Congress convened in Philadelphia.

09-17.text: Delegates signed the U.S. Constitution at the Philadelphia Convention.

09-24.text: The Judiciary Act of 1789 established the basic structure of the federal court system.

09-25.text: Congress proposed the amendments that became the Bill of Rights.
09-25.ap_connection: Civil liberties; amendment process; Anti-Federalist concerns

10-03.text: George Washington issued the first presidential Thanksgiving proclamation.

10-12.text: The USS Cole attack later shaped debates over presidential war powers and national security policy.
10-12.ap_connection: Foreign policy; executive power; national security

10-15.text: The Senate confirmed Clarence Thomas to the Supreme Court after highly contested hearings.
10-15.ap_connection: Judicial appointments; Senate confirmation; public opinion

10-19.text: British surrender at Yorktown effectively ended major fighting in the American Revolution.

10-26.text: The USA PATRIOT Act was signed, expanding federal surveillance and law-enforcement powers after September 11.

11-04.text: Barack Obama was elected president, becoming the first Black president of the United States.

11-07.text: Jeannette Rankin became the first woman elected to Congress.

11-08.text: John F. Kennedy was elected president in one of the closest elections in U.S. history.

11-09.text: The fall of the Berlin Wall reshaped U.S. foreign policy debates at the end of the Cold War.

11-19.text: Abraham Lincoln delivered the Gettysburg Address, redefining the Civil War around equality and democratic government.
11-19.ap_connection: Founding ideals; popular sovereignty; civil rights

11-22.text: President John F. Kennedy was assassinated, and Lyndon B. Johnson became president.
11-22.ap_connection: Presidential succession; executive branch; public trust

12-01.text: Rosa Parks was arrested in Montgomery, Alabama, helping spark the Montgomery Bus Boycott.
12-01.ap_connection: Civil rights; social movements; equal protection

12-05.text: The 21st Amendment was ratified, repealing Prohibition.

12-07.text: Japan attacked Pearl Harbor, leading Congress to declare war the next day.

12-10.text: Wyoming Territory granted women the right to vote, decades before the 19th Amendment.

12-15.text: The Bill of Rights was ratified, protecting key civil liberties against the national government.
12-15.ap_connection: Bill of Rights; civil liberties; amendments

12-18.text: The 13th Amendment was ratified, abolishing slavery in the United States.
12-18.ap_connection: Civil rights; Reconstruction amendments; federal power

12-19.text: The House of Representatives impeached President Bill Clinton.
